topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notification blinked on my phone, an unexpected notification for a Tuesday afternoon. A package. Addressed to Apartment 3B. I’d lived here for three years, and 3B had been occupied by an elderly gentleman who moved out last month. I walked to the door, my heart giving a strange little flutter. The box was plain brown cardboard, taped shut with heavy, unfamiliar silver wire. I signed for it, the postal worker looking vaguely uncomfortable as he handed over the confirmation slip. When I finally got it inside, the silence of the apartment amplified the sound of the tape tearing. Inside, nestled in packing peanuts, was a single, antique key, tarnished brass and intricately carved. I turned it over in my palm, a cold prickle running up my arm. Who would think this was meant for me?

[story 2]

I found the parcel tucked behind the recycling bin, a heavy, rectangular object that felt oddly dense. The shipping label was faded, the handwriting looping and elegant, certainly not mine. Apartment 3B. I frowned, peering at the address as if it held some hidden code. I carried it to the kitchen table, setting it down carefully. As I peeled back the packing tape, a faint, sweet scent—like dried lavender and old paper—wafted out. Inside, there wasn't anything valuable, just a folded, brittle piece of parchment bearing a single, cryptic drawing of a constellation I didn't recognize. I reread the script, my brow furrowing deeper with every line. It made no sense, yet it felt profoundly significant. I paced the small living room, running a hand through my hair, trying to reconcile the object with the empty space where the previous tenant used to live.

[story 3]

The doorbell rang, announcing a delivery. I opened the door to find a package resting on the welcome mat. It was addressed to the former resident of 3B. A sudden stillness settled over me; my breath hitched in my throat. I took the box inside, my movements slow and deliberate. The packaging was luxurious—thick, cream-colored paper sealed with a wax stamp bearing a crest I couldn't decipher. Inside, wrapped in tissue paper so delicate it seemed to float, was a small, perfectly preserved feather, iridescent blue and shimmering faintly in the hallway light. I held it up to the window, examining the way the light caught the barbules. I felt a deep sense of disorientation, a sudden shift in the atmosphere of my small space. It was as if someone had left a carefully curated secret behind, and I was the unwitting custodian of whatever it was.
